food_name: Kajmak (clotted cream, small portion, without bread)
region_of_origin: Western Balkans (Serbia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, North Macedonia, Montenegro)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301116 covers clotted cream as a dairy product category. Kajmak is a Balkan-style clotted cream; no kajmak-specific FoodOn term exists, so closest_parent is used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Cream) is the closest defensible parent. No kajmak-specific FoodEx2 code exists in the published matrix. Kajmak is a high-fat dairy cream product; A00LC is used as the nearest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226760005 (Cream) is the closest available concept. No kajmak-specific SNOMED CT code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A small serving of kajmak is approximately 50 g, typically presented as a generous dollop or spread alongside grilled meats or flatbread, though this record covers the kajmak alone without bread.

nutrient_source.title: Cream, sour (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 01056) and Cream, heavy whipping (NDB 01053) — adapted composite for kajmak
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No kajmak-specific entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. USDA SR Legacy heavy cream (NDB 01053, ~340 kcal/100 g, fat ~36 g) and sour cream (NDB 01056, ~198 kcal/100 g, fat ~19 g) were reviewed. Kajmak is a high-fat Balkan clotted cream with fat content typically reported in the range of 40–60 g/100 g in food composition literature (Dozet et al., 1996; Miloradovic et al., 2012). A representative mid-range composition of approximately 50 g fat/100 g was used, consistent with USDA heavy cream and peer-reviewed Balkan dairy literature. Values are estimated from the closest approved source (USDA SR Legacy heavy cream NDB 01053) and adjusted to reflect the slightly higher fat and lower water content typical of kajmak. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Dairy fat / cream analogous estimate.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kajmak exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Kajmak is a very high-fat, very low-carbohydrate dairy product (carbohydrate_g ~2.8/100 g). The GI of full-fat dairy cream products is consistently low (GI ~27 for full-fat dairy; Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). A GI of 27 is used as the analogous estimate for kajmak based on its compositional similarity to heavy cream. Glycemic load per 50 g serving = (27 × 1.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.4, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content and high saturated fat intake
hypertension.guidance: Kajmak contains approximately 40 mg sodium per 100 g and is high in saturated fat (22.5 g/100 g); individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and frequency of consumption, particularly when kajmak is served alongside high-sodium grilled meats or bread.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kajmak has a very low carbohydrate content (~2.8 g/100 g) and a negligible glycemic load per serving (~0.4 GL units), making it unlikely to cause acute postprandial glucose excursions; however, its high saturated fat content warrants attention in the context of insulin resistance and cardiovascular co-morbidity in type 2 diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kajmak is very high in saturated fat (~22.5 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~110 mg/100 g), which can raise LDL cholesterol; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should consume kajmak only occasionally and in small portions, consistent with current dietary guidelines limiting saturated fat to less than 10% of total energy intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kajmak is a traditional Balkan high-fat clotted cream made by slowly heating full-fat cow's milk, skimming the cream layer that forms on the surface, lightly salting it, and allowing it to rest and mature for hours to days. The result is a rich, thick, slightly tangy dairy spread with a buttery, mildly salty flavour and a dense, unctuous texture that is softer than butter but firmer than whipped cream. It is a staple condiment and accompaniment in Serbian, Bosnian, Montenegrin, and North Macedonian cuisine, most commonly served alongside grilled meats such as ćevapi, pljeskavica, and lamb chops, or spread on warm flatbread (lepinja or somun). This record covers kajmak served as a small standalone portion without bread, as offered at Balkan and Middle Eastern restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, kajmak is very high in fat (approximately 36 g/100 g, predominantly saturated), low in carbohydrate (~2.8 g/100 g), and moderate in protein (~2.1 g/100 g), yielding approximately 345 kcal per 100 g. Its glycemic index is low (~27) and glycemic load per small serving is negligible (~0.4), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. However, its high saturated fat and cholesterol content make it a food of concern for cardiovascular disease risk. It is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian.
common_user_queries: Is kajmak the same as kaymak?
Is kajmak good for keto diet?
Is kajmak similar to sour cream?

confidence_reason: No kajmak-specific entry exists in any primary regional approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy heavy cream (NDB 01053), which is the closest approved source, with adjustments informed by peer-reviewed Balkan dairy composition literature (Dozet et al., 1996; Miloradovic et al., 2012). GI is an analogous estimate from full-fat dairy cream data in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy heavy cream (NDB 01053) as the closest approved source; no kajmak-specific entry exists in primary regional databases. Values reflect a representative mid-range kajmak composition. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: dairy cream contains no dietary fibre per USDA SR Legacy. 3. GI of 27 is an analogous estimate based on full-fat dairy cream; no direct kajmak GI measurement is available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. The input record classifies this food under 'Middle Eastern' cuisine and 'Mixed' food group; these have been corrected to 'Balkan' cuisine and 'Dairy and eggs' food group to reflect the food's actual origin and composition. The restaurant (Sahara Bar & Restaurant) serves Middle Eastern and Balkan cuisine, and kajmak is a Balkan product. 5. Serving size of 50 g is estimated for a small restaurant portion without bread. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 01053 (heavy cream) as the closest approved source; these are not directly measured for kajmak. 7. This food is not a Singaporean dish; therefore the Singaporean multilingual naming requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. The cuisine is Balkan/Eastern European and no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under the Veritas multilingual matrix for this cuisine category. Romanized local names (kajmak, kaymak) are provided. 8. Halal status is listed as true based on the product being a plain dairy cream with no pork-derived ingredients; however, formal halal certification of the specific restaurant preparation is not confirmed.
